key_insight: Machine-learned dynamics reveal that small polarons at oxygen-deficient rutile TiO2(110) move far more slowly than in the bulk because surface hopping pathways are severely restricted.
engineering_relevance: This helps explain why porous rutile TiO2 can show lower electron mobility than single-crystal material, informing the design of oxide surfaces for catalysis, photovoltaics, batteries, and energy storage.
method: Extended machine-learning-assisted polaron dynamics to a redox-active oxide surface.
Simulated oxygen-deficient rutile TiO2(110) for several nanoseconds across temperatures.
Analyzed polaron hopping pathways and vacancy-induced free-energy bias.
evidence_signal: Nanosecond-scale simulations show surface polaron mobility is suppressed by several orders of magnitude relative to bulk, with mainly planar inter-row hopping and only rare interlayer events.
watch_item: The approach is powerful but depends on a trained machine-learning model and careful transferability checks when moving to other oxide surfaces or defect environments.
paper_title: Machine-learned dynamics of surface polarons at reduced oxide surfaces
